metadata.title: The Copy-Paste Gamble: How Borrowed Mechanics Fail
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: The Copy-Paste Gamble: How Borrowed Mech
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. ChatGPT produced a transform-translation controller that moves a GameObject directly across the screen. The code moves along Vector3.right, which is world-space positive X, and requires no Rigidbody2D component. The controller compiles and runs, but it sends the object in a wrong direction for the intended design. This is not a story about AI being wrong; it is about borrowed mechanics failing silently in a new context.

artifactLines: ChatGPT produced a transform-translation controller that moves a GameObject directly across the screen.
The code moves along Vector3.right, which is world-space positive X, and requires no Rigidbody2D component.
The controller compiles and runs, but it sends the object in a wrong direction for the intended design.
This is not a story about AI being wrong; it is about borrowed mechanics failing silently in a new context.

beats.narration_text: The detail that ChatGPT produced a transform-translation controller moving the GameObject along Vector3.right, world-space positive X, with no Rigidbody2D, but in a wrong direction really stuck with me. I want to understand exactly why borrowed code compiles yet misbehaves. Can you (1) write both the transform-translate version and a Rigidbody2D physics version side by side, (2) explain when world-space X is the wrong axis for a given camera or player orientation, and (3) list three test cases that would catch this bug before it ships?
